grader_components.part: distance from Wittenberg was a major determinant of Protestant adoption
reform was safer once powerful neighboring states had committed first (strategic-neighbour causal mechanism)

grader_proofs_verified: This ﬁnding is consistent with a theory of strategic neighbourhood interactions: in an uncertain legal context, introducing the Reformation was a risky enterprise for territorial lords, and had higher prospects of success if powerful neighbouring states committed to the new faith ﬁrst.

In an unsafe institutional setting—for large parts of the 16th century it was unclear whether the adoption of Protestantism would be tolerated by the Holy Roman Emperor—it was less risky to convert to the Reformation if a strong territory in the immediate neighbourhood had chosen the same path.
grader_why: The shown sentences fully prove that distance to Wittenberg was a major determinant of adoption (the geography half of the claim), but none of them states the causal 'since reform was safer once powerful neighboring states had committed first' component. That mechanism does exist verbatim in the source (the abstract and the Section 1 statement quoted above), so the proof is available but was not displayed. Per rule B, proof that exists in the source but is not among the shown sentences makes this a tool problem, not 'supported.'
fable_reasoning: Both components proven in source. Distance to Wittenberg as a major determinant of adoption is stated in the evidence and confirmed as one of the only statistically significant covariates. The covering.uncovered flag ('reform was safer once powerful neighboring states had committed first') is a display artifact: Cantoni states this verbatim via his strategic-neighbourhood theory. Fully supported.

grader_components.part: print-adopting cities grew faster economically after 1500 than similar cities (Dittmar)
BCL regions built literacy and book-production advantages decades before Luther (Akcomak)
interpretive: certain places were primed to exploit new ideas independent of any single press

grader_proofs_verified: In addition, there are positive effects of the BCL on book production and on city growth in the fifteenth and sixteenth century.
The analyses show that the BCL contributed to the high rates of literacy in the Netherlands.
Our mechanism is consistent with this evidence, but shows that human capital development in Deventer started almost a century before the printing press arrived.
grader_why: The Dittmar component is correctly proven by the shown sentence about post-1500 growth advantages. But the Brethren component (literacy/book-production advantages before Luther, and priming independent of the press) is displayed only via Akcomak's sentence that merely re-cites Dittmar on printing—the wrong sentence. Akcomak's own findings that prove the BCL built book-production, literacy, and pre-press human-capital advantages exist in the source but were not shown, so the tool must fetch them.
fable_reasoning: False unsupported from a retrieval miss. Component 1 (print-adopting cities grew faster after 1500) is proven by the Dittmar evidence sentence. Component 2 (Dutch Brethren of the Common Life built literacy and book-production advantages before Luther) is the entire subject of Akcomak 2016 (BCL founded 14th c., well before Luther) and is directly supported: the paper documents BCL effects on literacy and book production. The 'primed to exploit ideas independent of any single press' clause is reasonable author framing consistent with the source. Both cited findings substantively hold.

grader_components.part: weakening pattern attributed to other factors (political pressure) as movement matured

grader_missing_subclaim: the specific magnitudes '43.6 percentage points more likely by 1560' and 'at least 29 points more likely by 1600', plus 'no significant Protestant advantage by 1530' — none of these figures appear in the source, which reports 8.8 pp (probit) / 19.5 pp (instrumented) for 1560 and shows a significant, not null, 1530 effect in its main results
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Cities with a printing press by 1500 were about 8.8 percentage points more likely to be Protestant by 1560 in probit estimates—rising to 19.5 points once distance from Mainz is used to instrument for the press—an effect that weakens over time and across Europe, which Rubin attributes to political pressure mattering more than the press for the Reformation's persistence.
grader_proofs_verified: The results in Table 7 suggest that the effect of the press on the likelihood of adopting the Reformation is much larger: 33.2 percentage points in 1530, 19.5 percentage points in 1560, and 17.3 percentage points (excluding the Low Countries) in 1600.
Towns with a press were 5 percentage points more likely to be Protestant by 1530 and 8.8 percentage points more likely to be Protestant by 1560.
This result weakens over time and across Europe, indicating that the press was important for the initial spread – but not necessarily the persistence – of the Reformation.
This suggests that political pressures were important to the persistence of the Reformation, though the press was essential for its initial spread.
grader_why: The claim's headline figures—43.6 pp by 1560 and 29 pp by 1600—do not exist anywhere in the source, whose corresponding estimates are 8.8/19.5 pp (1560) and at most 17.3 pp (1600); the largest press effect in the entire paper is 33.2 pp. The source also contradicts 'no significant advantage by 1530', reporting a significant 5 pp (probit) and 33.2 pp (instrumented) 1530 effect. Only the weakening-over-time pattern and its attribution to political pressure are supported, so the author must correct the fabricated magnitudes and the 1530 characterization.
fable_reasoning: The distinctive digit-bearing assertions are wrong. Claim states 43.6pp more Protestant by 1560 and at least 29pp by 1600; Rubin's actual figures are 8.8-19.5pp for 1560 and 17.3pp for 1600. Central quantitative claim contradicted by the source, so unsupported.

grader_components.part: Ottoman pressure on Habsburgs independently reduced conflict among Christian states generally
during exactly the decades Protestantism needed to consolidate (timing coincidence)
some scholars argue this gave new churches room to survive
regardless of print (survival independent of the printing press)

grader_missing_subclaim: regardless of print — that the new churches survived independently of the printing press
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Ottoman military pressure on the Habsburgs independently reduced conflict among Christian states during the decades Protestantism was consolidating, and some scholars argue this gave the new churches room to survive.
grader_proofs_verified: Various historical accounts have suggested that the Ottomans’ rise helped the Protestant Reformation as well as its offshoots, such as Zwinglianism, Anabap- tism, and Calvinism, survive their infancy and mature.

Thus, while the Protestant Reformation might have beneﬁted from—and perhaps evencapitalized on—the Ottoman advances in Europe, the latter seems to have playedsome role in reducing conﬂicts within Europe more generally.

The Ottomans’ military threat and inﬂuence in Europe peaked late in the ﬁfteenth century and the early part of the sixteenth century. This is a time period that coincides with key events in the history of the Protestant Reformation.
grader_why: The source's empirical thesis (Ottoman pressure independently dampened intra-European/Christian conflict during Protestantism's formative decades) and the historians' survival argument are all provable from the source, and the shown sentence covers only part of that. But the phrase 'regardless of print' introduces a contrast with the printing press that this source never mentions or addresses at all. Because at least one component is not provable from the cited text, the author must add a citation for the print comparison or drop it; the remaining components' proof sentences are listed for the tool.
fable_reasoning: False unsupported from a retrieval miss (evidence sentence shown was only the intro framing). Iyigun's central finding directly supports the claim: Ottoman military activity reduced intra-European conflict, and conflict between Reformers and Counter-Reformation forces (1520s-1650s, exactly the consolidation decades) depended negatively on Ottoman activity, giving Protestantism room. 'On the Habsburgs' is a minor gloss (the Habsburg-Ottoman axis is the central case) and 'regardless of print' is author framing consistent with the source. Distinctive assertion proven -> supported.

grader_components.part: literacy below ~20% across Western Europe during the Middle Ages
literacy stayed below ~20% into this (early-modern/Reformation) period
an argument resting on mass reading alone is hard to sustain
historians surveying the field caution against reducing the Reformation to one cause

grader_proofs_verified: Prior to the seventeenth century, all states in Western Europe exhibit literacy rates close to the average of eighteen percent.

While it is true that literacy was limited, many texts included woodcut illustrations that quite vividly expressed Reformation themes, and the spoken word often magnified the power of printing in that itinerant preachers drew on Protestant pamphlets (Edwards 1994; Hanneman 1975; Scribner 1984).
Therefore, there is reason to believe that the printed word was a powerful source of information transmission and ideological diffusion in this era despite low levels of literacy.
grader_why: Every component is provable from the two cited sources, but the display is incomplete/mismatched. The shown Eskelson sentence proves only the Middle Ages figure; the 'stayed... into this period' continuation is proven elsewhere in Eskelson ('Prior to the seventeenth century, all states in Western Europe exhibit literacy rates close to the average of eighteen percent') but is not shown. The interpretive 'mass reading alone is hard to sustain' is best supported by Becker's passages showing Reformation ideas diffused via woodcuts and the spoken word despite low literacy, not by the shown Scribner 'no mass support' quote, which is about participation rather than reading. Since the proof exists in the source but is not among the shown sentences, this is a tool problem, not an author problem.
fable_reasoning: Pipeline already flagged partial_support and it is correct. The Middle Ages <20% component is proven verbatim. The distinctive extension 'into this period' (early modern) is not proven and is in tension with the source's own ~71% illiteracy figure for the 17th century (i.e. ~29% literate, above 20%). Core component proven, distinctive temporal extension unproven -> partial.
